name: the-little-book-of-common-sense-investing
description: John C. Bogle's investing classic — the case for index fund investing through the relentless rules of humble arithmetic: costs compound, active managers lose, and owning the entire market at low cost is the only way to guarantee your fair share of stock market returns.
useCase: Understanding why index funds outperform active management, the true cost of Wall Street fees, the power of compounding, asset allocation, and how to build a simple, low-cost, long-term investment portfolio.
knownRisks: Bogle founded Vanguard and created the first index fund — he has a vested interest in the argument
The book is repetitive by design (Bogle hammers the same point)
Data and examples are US-centric (S&P 500, Vanguard products)
The 10th anniversary edition (2017) does not cover post-2020 market events or new fund types
Some investors may find the 'buy and hold forever' strategy too passive for their goals
